expert_chain:
Round 1
There are an abundant tropical vegetation with Southeast Asian architecture (metal roof and wall architecture) 
The road is made of concrete, which is mainly found in Thailand and Philippines in Southeast Asia, but the crossing sign is set for right hand drive, making Thailand not possible (since they drive left hand side)
English street names are also not found in Southeast Asian countries asides from Philippines
Wooden poles are not unique to Philippines, but commonly found there
Oil palms are found majority in the region of Mindanao, the southern main island
The tuktuk in the distance seems to be in the same configuration as southern tuktuks with one one main cabin and not two sections (which are the most common in northern and central Philippines)
Conclusion
This location is 100% in Philippines, likely in Mindanao or any regions in central/southern Philippines

Round 2
Northern foliage (some type of fur or pine trees) with North American architecture makes only northern USA and Canada possible
Double yellow lines is more common in USA, but could be found in Canadian states such as but not limited to Quebec and Nova Scotia
Electricity pole top with a long cross bar is found in Quebec and states west of Quebec
Long curved lamp support is commonly seen in Quebec, but is not definitive to Quebec
Strict north-south grid is more characteristic of prairies states like Manitoba and Saskatchewan
The architecture looks rather ambiguous and not characteristic of Quebec or a prairies state
The insulator attached to top of the electricity pole does not appear to be facing towards the road, ruling out Quebec which normally has road facing middle insulators 
Conclusion
This location is highly likely in Canada, and is a tossup between Quebec and a prairies state like Manitoba or Saskatchewan

Round 3
A straight road with single yellow centerline is indicative of Latin American countries as North American countries majorly use double yellow center lines
The road quality with tire marks and potential tar is very indicative of US and Mexico
The dryness of the climate with yellow shrubbery is common along the regions of the US and Mexico border
Wooden fences are not as common in US, as they use mainly painted metal fences
Low rocky mountains in the distance fits for states in Mexico such as Chihuahua and Coahuila
Conclusion
This location is very likely to be in the states of Chihuahua and Coahuila in Mexico


Round 4
The shop sign on the right side of the road is in Vietnamese
Vietnam drives on the right side of the road, which is confirmed with the direction the shop sign is placed (right side of the road)
Abundance of tropical vegetation such as bamboo and banana plants combined with reddish exposed mountains fits for northern Vietnam 
Conclusion
This location is 100% in Vietnam, likely in Northern Vietnam (regions North of Hanoi)

Round 5
Landscape and vegetation is typical of Southern Patagonia, with low dry shrubbery and medium sides mountains
Exposed white back of the google car is only found in Chile, Peru, and Bolivia in South America
Yellow sign with wooden thin sign posts is not found in Peru and Bolivia, but is the typical style found in Chile
Wooden spaced stick fences are found in Southern Patagonia as well
The aridness and dirt road coverage leads to the area around Puerto Natales as similar landscapes south of Puerto Natales normally has triple solid road lines on paved roads
Conclusion
This location is 100% in Chile, highly likely in the Southern Patagonia regions around Puerto Natales

prediction.predicted_address: Residential junction in New Amsterdam, East Berbice-Corentyne, Guyana

Through Skill 1's region-support edges, Skills 4, 5, 10, and 12 still keep South America as the best broad regional frame because the road environment is compatible with a Latin American coastal settlement even without strong lane paint. Skill 2 suggests rural Vietnam, but the streetscape, architecture, and utility layout do not fit Vietnam well. Its linked cue-composition edges to Skills 4, 6, 7, 8, 10, 11, and 12 do not improve those alternatives. Skill 3 suggests Indonesian residential neighborhoods, but the built environment and road style are less convincing for Indonesia. Skill 4 again favors Latin America, though the expected double yellow line is not visible. Skill 5 references a small yellow warning sign similar in Colombia or Ecuador; there is a yellow pedestrian-style warning sign present, which supports a general Latin American interpretation more than the Asian or European alternatives. Skill 6 points to Australia or New Zealand because of a wide smooth unmarked residential road, but the tropical architecture, dense overhead wiring, and house styles argue against that. Skill 7 points to Belgian villages due to concrete paving, but the climate and buildings clearly do not fit Belgium. Skill 8 points to the United States or Mexico, but the road marking pattern is absent. Skill 9 points to Japan based on yellow sign design, but the rest of the scene strongly disagrees. Skill 10 supports Latin America from faint yellow striping, though striping is weak here. Skill 11 points to Russia or post-Soviet areas, which is incompatible with the tropical low-rise setting. Skill 12 supports South America, but its exact marking pattern is also not visible. After following the graph, the strongest surviving regional conclusion is South America, while the observed left-driving-style roadside setup, tropical coastal residential character, wooden and corrugated homes, pale concrete streets, and Guyana-like utility infrastructure make Guyana the best country fit. Within Guyana, the scene matches a residential junction in New Amsterdam, East Berbice-Corentyne.",
  "evidence": [
    "Flat humid tropical residential environment",
    "Low-rise wooden and corrugated-metal houses",
    "Wide pale concrete road with minimal lane markings",
    "Dense overhead utility wiring on simple poles",
    "Yellow pedestrian-style warning sign",
    "Quiet residential junction layout consistent with coastal Guyana",
    "Overall South American fit stronger than Asian, European, or Oceanian alternatives"
  ]
}

Dense overhead utility wiring on simple poles
Quiet residential junction layout consistent with coastal Guyana
Overall South American fit stronger than Asian, European, or Oceanian alternatives

retrieved_skills.skill_text: The road markings are simple double yellow lines in the middle, common in Brazil and many other Latin American countries.
The overall condition and layout of the road fit rural areas in Vietnam.
The road is narrow and paved with simple asphalt, a feature often seen in Indonesian residential neighborhoods.
The road has double yellow center lines and a slight curve, commonly seen in Latin American countries, especially in Colombia and Ecuador.
No visible language on signage, but the small yellow curve warning sign is similar in shape and color to those used in Colombia and Ecuador.
The road is wide, smooth, and lacks any lane markings, a common feature in the quiet residential cul-de-sacs of Australian and New Zealand suburbs.
The road is paved with concrete and has narrow shoulders, a feature seen in Belgian villages.
The road has a single dashed yellow centerline with solid white outside edges, a marking pattern common in the United States and Mexico.
The yellow road sign shape and design are characteristic of Japanese road signage.
The road features a faint yellow centerline striping, which is standard in Latin American countries including Brazil and Mexico.
The road is very wide with a simple dashed white center line and solid white shoulder lines, a style common in Russia and nearby post-Soviet countries.
The road markings feature double yellow lines in the center and dashed white lines on the sides, typical of South American countries, especially Argentina.
